decision_summary: The design is feasible with a chip max temperature of 58.6°C, well below the 85.0°C constraint. However, the optimization history indicates diminishing returns from geometry changes and suggests shifting to material tuning. Since the heat spreader conductivity is at its upper bound (500.0), the next most effective lever is to increase the base plate conductivity to improve downstream heat extraction toward the side sinks.

changes.reason: Doubling the base plate conductivity (within the allowed 2.0x ratio) reduces thermal resistance between the heat spreader and the lateral Dirichlet boundaries, facilitating better heat removal now that spreader improvements are saturated.

expected_effects: Flatter temperature gradient across the base plate
risk_notes: Diminishing returns may occur if the bottleneck shifts entirely to the spreader-base interface contact resistance (not modeled here) or if the side boundaries become the limiting factor regardless of bulk conductivity.
Material cost or weight implications in a real-world scenario (though not tracked in this simulation).
